title: Calycophyllum coccineum
taxonomy: Calycophyllum coccineum is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Rubiaceae. It belongs to the genus Calycophyllum, which is a group of tropical trees native to Central and South America. The species Calycophyllum coccineum is further classified as a member of the order Gentianales and the class Magnoliopsida. The classification of Calycophyllum coccineum is as follows: Kingdom - Plantae, Division - Magnoliophyta, Class - Magnoliopsida, Order - Gentianales, Family - Rubiaceae, Genus - Calycophyllum, Species - Calycophyllum coccineum. The exact classification may vary slightly depending on the source.
uses: Calycophyllum coccineum has been traditionally used in folk medicine for various purposes. It is reported to have antibacterial, antifungal, and anti-inflammatory properties, which are beneficial in treating wounds and infections. The plant's extracts are also believed to have antimalarial and antiparasitic activities, making it a potential remedy for certain tropical diseases. Additionally, the plant is used in traditional medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, diarrhea, and respiratory issues. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of Calycophyllum coccineum for medicinal use.

intro: Calycophyllum coccineum, also known as the scarlet tree or bloodwood tree, is a plant species belonging to the family Rubiaceae, native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiparasitic properties, which may aid in the treatment of various health conditions such as fever, skin infections, and gastrointestinal issues. The bioactive constituents of Calycophyllum coccineum include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and alkaloids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is commonly used to prepare herbal teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat fever, cough, and respiratory issues.
